aestheticusability-effect: The tendency to treat attractive things as more usable than they really are.
anthropomorphism: The tendency to treat animals, objects, or abstractions as if they had human thoughts, feelings, or intentions.
apophenia: The tendency to perceive meaningful connections or patterns between unrelated things.
assumed-similarity-bias: The tendency to assume other people are more similar to oneself than they really are.
attribute-substitution: The tendency to answer a hard judgment question by unconsciously substituting an easier one.
availability-cascade: A belief becoming more plausible through repeated public repetition, social uptake, and feedback.
ballot-order-effect: The tendency for candidates listed first on a ballot to gain a small voting advantage.
barnum-effect: The tendency to accept vague, flattering, or generic descriptions as uniquely accurate of oneself.
ben-franklin-effect: The tendency to like or help someone more after already doing that person a favor.
berksons-paradox: The tendency to draw misleading statistical conclusions from conditionally selected samples.
bizarreness-effect: The tendency to remember bizarre or unusual material better than ordinary material.
compromise-effect-choices-affected-if-pr: The tendency for an option to seem better when it appears as a middle compromise.
cryptomnesia: The tendency to mistake an old memory or borrowed idea for a new original thought.
cue-dependent-forgetting: The tendency for recall to weaken when the original context or cues are missing.
curse-of-knowledge: The tendency for better-informed people to underestimate how hard the issue looks to less-informed people.
decoy-effect: The tendency for a dominated third option to shift preference toward a nearby target option.
domain-neglect: The tendency to ignore relevant domain knowledge when reasoning across unfamiliar fields.
doubling-back-aversion: The tendency to resist restarting or retracing steps even when doing so would save time or effort.
egocentric-bias: The tendency to remember the past in self-serving ways and overweight one's own perspective.
experimenters-bias: The tendency for researchers' expectations to shape what data they notice, trust, publish, or discount.
extension-neglect: The tendency to judge outcomes without giving enough weight to sample size or quantity.
false-memory: The tendency to mistake imagination, suggestion, or reconstruction for an actual memory.
fluency-heuristic: The tendency to treat ideas or options that feel easier to process as better or truer.
frequency-illusion: The tendency to notice something once and then feel as if it is suddenly everywhere.
generation-effect: The tendency to remember self-generated information better than information supplied by others.
group-attribution-error: The tendency to project one member's traits or a group decision onto the whole group.
groupthink: The tendency for groups to protect harmony or momentum at the cost of critical evaluation and dissent.
hedonic-recall-bias: The tendency for current satisfaction to distort how positively or negatively past experience is remembered.
humor-effect: The tendency to remember humorous material more easily than comparable non-humorous material.
hyperbolic-discounting: The tendency to prefer smaller immediate rewards over larger later ones, even against one's longer-term interests.
illusion-of-asymmetric-insight: The tendency to think one understands others better than they understand oneself.
illusion-of-transparency: The tendency to overestimate how visible one's thoughts, feelings, or intentions are to others.
implicit-association: The tendency for response speed to reflect how strongly ideas are linked in memory.
insensitivity-to-sample-size: The tendency to under-expect variation in small samples and overread sparse data.
interoceptive-bias: The tendency for hunger, fatigue, pain, or other bodily states to distort judgment.
leveling-and-sharpening: The tendency for memories to lose some details while exaggerating others as they are retold over time.
levels-of-processing-effect: The tendency for deeper, more meaningful encoding to produce stronger memory than shallow encoding.
loss-aversion: The tendency for losses or giving something up to feel worse than equivalent gains feel good.
misinterpreted-effort-hypothesis: The tendency to interpret learning effort as a sign of poor learning rather than durable learning.
modality-effect: The tendency for the last items in a list to be remembered better when heard than when read.
motonormativity: The tendency to treat motor vehicle use as normal while discounting harms that would stand out in other contexts.
naive-realism: The tendency to see one's own view as plain reality and disagreement as ignorance, bias, or irrationality.
naive-cynicism: The tendency to expect more egocentric bias in others than in oneself.
next-in-line-effect: The tendency to remember less of the person who spoke just before one's own turn.
non-adaptive-choice-switching: The tendency to avoid a previously optimal choice after a bad outcome, even when the situation is unchanged.
observer-expectancy-effect: The tendency for a researcher's expectations to unconsciously shape procedures, observations, or interpretations.
outcome-bias: The tendency to judge a decision mainly by its result rather than by the quality of the reasoning behind it.
outgroup-favoritism: The tendency for some disadvantaged groups to evaluate outside groups more favorably than their own.
outgroup-homogeneity-bias: The tendency to see members of other groups as more alike than members of one's own group.
pareidolia: The tendency to perceive meaningful patterns, faces, or messages in vague or random stimuli.
part-list-cueing-effect: The tendency for seeing some items from a list to make the remaining items harder to recall.
peakend-rule: The tendency to remember an experience mainly by its peak moment and its ending.
phantom-effect: The tendency for unavailable but mentally present options to influence current choices.
picture-superiority-effect: The tendency to remember pictured information better than the same information presented only as words.
placement-bias: The tendency to remember oneself as above average at strengths and below average at weaknesses.
persistence: The tendency for traumatic memories to recur unwantedly after the event is over.
prevention-bias: The tendency to overvalue prevention spending relative to equally effective detection or response.
primacy-effect: The tendency to remember items at the beginning of a sequence especially well.
processing-difficulty-effect: The tendency to remember information better when processing it required more effort or thought.
puritanical-bias: The tendency to explain wrongdoing as personal moral failure rather than as partly shaped by social conditions.
rhyme-as-reason-effect: The tendency to treat rhyming statements as more truthful or convincing.
salience-bias: The tendency to focus on striking or emotional information and neglect less vivid but relevant information.
scope-neglect: The tendency to respond weakly to scale, treating small and large harms or benefits as if the difference barely matters.
selective-perception: The tendency for expectations or prior beliefs to shape what one notices or perceives.
self-relevance-effect: The tendency to remember information better when it is connected to oneself.
serial-position-effect: The tendency to remember items at the beginning and end of a sequence better than those in the middle.
shared-information-bias: The tendency for groups to spend too much time discussing shared information and too little on unique information.
social-cryptomnesia: The tendency for societies to remember that change happened while forgetting who caused it and how.
spacing-effect: The tendency to remember information better when exposure is spaced out over time.
stereotype-bias-or-stereotypical-bias: The tendency for memory or judgment to drift toward familiar social stereotypes.
subjective-validation: The tendency to treat a claim as true because it fits one's beliefs, hopes, or personal experience.
suggestibility: The tendency to absorb suggested details and later misremember them as one's own memory.
system-justification: The tendency to defend, rationalize, and bolster the current social or institutional order.
tachypsychia: The tendency for time to feel slowed down or sped up during intense stress or arousal.
time-saving-bias: The tendency to misjudge how much time is saved by speed changes, especially at low versus high speeds.
tip-of-the-tongue: The tendency to partly retrieve a memory while remaining unable to pull up the needed word or item.
travis-syndrome: The tendency to overestimate the importance, uniqueness, or permanence of the present moment.
ultimate-attribution-error: The tendency to explain whole outgroups in dispositional terms while excusing ingroup members more situationally.
unit-bias: The tendency to treat a standard unit or serving as the right amount to consume.
verbatim-effect: The tendency to remember the gist of what was said better than the exact wording.
von-restorff-effect: The tendency to remember an item better when it stands out from its surroundings.
zeigarnik-effect: The tendency to remember unfinished or interrupted tasks better than completed ones.
zero-sum-bias: The tendency to see situations as zero-sum even when gains for one need not mean losses for another.
